posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: September 2025
posts.excerpt: September 2025 delivered huge sporting emotions — the World Athletics Championships and the Men's Volleyball World Championship — alongside a dramatic incident in Polish skies when Russian drones violating Polish airspace were shot down. Internationally, social protests swept several countries, and the Catholic Church witnessed a historic canonisation.
posts.body:
September 2025 delivered huge sporting emotions — the World Athletics Championships and the Men's Volleyball World Championship — alongside a dramatic incident in Polish skies when Russian drones violating Polish airspace were shot down. Internationally, social protests swept several countries, and the Catholic Church witnessed a historic canonisation.

## WORLD

### Politics & Security
* Parliamentary elections in Norway — a shift in the balance of political power.
* Sushila Karki becomes Prime Minister of Nepal following 'Generation Z' protests — the first woman to hold the office in the country's history.
* New Prime Minister of Lithuania — Inga Ruginienė of the Lithuanian Social Democratic Party, confirmed by a vote of 80/40/2.
* Mass protests across France — hundreds of thousands take to the streets, with an estimated one million in Paris on 18 September, opposing the government's 44 billion euro austerity package.
* Elections held in the Seychelles, Bougainville, and parliamentary-local elections in Gabon.
* Russia continues its aggression against Ukraine — strikes hit central Kyiv and government buildings; at least 4 killed, including an infant.

### Disasters & Accidents
* At least 107 people killed on the Congo River when a fishing vessel caught fire in the DRC's Equatorial Province; over 200 rescued.
* A train collides with a double-decker bus in Mexico — at least 10 dead, 41 injured.
* At least 9 dead after a rescue boat capsizes during flooding in Pakistan.

## POLAND

### Defence & Security
* Polish Air Force shoots down 4 Russian drones violating Polish airspace — the first direct Polish combat engagement since the war began. Airports including Warsaw-Modlin, Rzeszow-Jasionka, and Lublin temporarily closed.
* Prime Minister Donald Tusk invokes Article 4 of the North Atlantic Treaty — consultations launched with allies.

### Culture
* 50th Polish Film Festival in Gdynia — Golden Lions awarded to 'Ministranci' (The Altar Boys) directed by Piotr Domalewski.
* Rev. Marcin Orawski installed as bishop of the Wroclaw diocese of the Evangelical-Augsburg Church.
* Demolition of the historic Stradecki tenement at ul. Grzybowska 46 in Warsaw — a controversial decision by city authorities.

## SPORT & CULTURE

* World Athletics Championships 2025 — Tokyo; numerous world records and medals across the globe.
* Men's Volleyball World Championship — Philippines; Italy triumphs for the second consecutive time. MVP: Alessandro Michieletto.
* Italy also claims gold in the Women's Volleyball World Championship.
* US Open 2025 — Carlos Alcaraz (men's) and Aryna Sabalenka (women's) claim Grand Slam glory.
* KS Toruń defeats Speedway Lublin in the Speedway Extraleague final — the club's 5th Polish team championship.
* Pope Leo XIV presides over his first canonisation — declares Pier Giorgio Frassati and Carlo Acutis as saints.

## ECONOMY & TECHNOLOGY

* Silver prices climb to $42 per ounce — highest level in 14 years. UBS forecasts further rises to $47 by mid-2026.
* Inauguration of the Jupiter supercomputer in Juelich (24,000 Nvidia GH processors) — Europe's first system capable of training world-class AI models.

## SCIENCE & OTHER

* Total lunar eclipse — visible across Europe, Africa, Asia, and Australia.
* The Perseverance rover discovers vivianite, greigite, and organic compounds near the Cheyava waterfall on Mars — significant for the search for signs of life.

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: October 2025
posts.excerpt: October brought silence to Gaza — a ceasefire took hold after more than a year of fighting. China shocked the world with rare earth metal export controls, rattling global supply chains. In Poland, a corruption investigation reached the Mayor of Wroclaw, while a parliamentary committee scrutinised the NBP governor.
posts.body:
October brought silence to Gaza — a ceasefire took hold after more than a year of fighting. China shocked the world with rare earth metal export controls, rattling global supply chains. In Poland, a corruption investigation reached the Mayor of Wrocław, while a parliamentary committee scrutinised the NBP governor.

## WORLD

### Politics & Conflicts
* **10 OCTOBER —** A ceasefire between Israel and Hamas in the Gaza Strip comes into force. Fighting ceases, but the humanitarian crisis continues — approximately 1.6 million Gaza residents face famine.
* 22 October — The International Court of Justice calls on Israel to ensure humanitarian access and lift blockades. Aid organisations warn that deliveries remain severely obstructed.
* APEC Summit in Busan — Trump meets Xi Jinping on 30 October; talks focus on China's rare earth export controls.
* China introduces export controls on rare earth metals — any product containing even 1% of these materials requires a Chinese licence. Global supply chains face a serious disruption.
* Parliamentary elections in Czech Republic — centre-right coalition wins.
* Presidential election in Romania — held amid scandal, allegations of manipulation, and street protests.
* Incident at the Chernobyl nuclear exclusion zone following a Russian strike — blackout in the 'Ark' zone.
* EU leaders meeting in Copenhagen agree to build a 'drone wall' along Europe's eastern border.

## POLAND

### Politics & Law
* Jacek Sutryk, Mayor of Wrocław, faces corruption charges — the ruling Civic Coalition calls for his resignation.
* Constitutional Accountability Commission hearings on the potential responsibility of NBP Governor Adam Glapiński before the Tribunal of State.
* The government reviews changes to the Civil Protection and Civil Defence Programme — procurement of emergency equipment.
* A draft law on the Security and Defence Fund — Poland expected to receive approximately 4.65 billion euros from the RRF/KPO.
* Amendment to the Developer Act (from 9 September) — new rules on developer liability for construction defects.

### Security
* Interior Minister Marcin Kierwiński meets Baltic state interior ministers in Gdańsk — security coordination talks.
* The military is authorised to support the Border Guard — presidential decision issued.

## ECONOMY & TECHNOLOGY

* US imposes new sanctions on Russian oil — further tightening of restrictions on the Russian economy.
* Discovery of large copper deposits in Poland — significant news for the domestic mining industry.
* Chinese rare earth export controls become the number-one story for global markets.
* Record US budget deficit in October — $284.3 billion, surpassing the peak Covid-19 crisis figure.
* Gold stabilises after earlier record highs; silver holds near multi-year peaks.

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: November 2025
posts.excerpt: November was defined in Poland by a railway sabotage incident and political turbulence — a change of Sejm Speaker electrified public opinion. Globally, the US budget crisis deepened and the DOGE programme began to quietly wind down.
posts.body:
November was defined in Poland by a railway sabotage incident and political turbulence — a change of Sejm Speaker electrified public opinion. Globally, the US budget crisis deepened and the DOGE programme began to quietly wind down.

## WORLD

### Politics & Geopolitics
* Ongoing peace negotiations regarding Ukraine — Trump balances pressure on Kyiv with threats of sanctions against Russia.
* Australia passes legislation banning major social media platforms from granting access to users under 16 — comes into force in December.
* Over 20,000 schools closed in Nigeria following a wave of student and teacher kidnappings.
* Norway launches a search operation for soldiers missing after military exercises.
* Netanyahu faces mounting international criticism for blocking humanitarian aid deliveries to Gaza.

## POLAND

### Politics & Security
* RAILWAY SABOTAGE — an explosive device damages railway tracks near Życzyno (Masovian Voivodeship). A major rail disaster is narrowly averted.
* CHANGE OF SEJM SPEAKER — a political watershed within the ruling coalition; intense public debate follows.
* Poland excluded from direct talks on Ukraine — diplomatic tensions with key players in the negotiations.
* President Nawrocki continues to veto legislation — growing friction between the presidency and the government.

## ECONOMY & TECHNOLOGY

* Record US budget deficit at the start of the fiscal year — $284.3 billion in October alone.
* DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) gradually loses momentum — the programme's 'quiet end'.
* Dramatic fall in real estate investment in China — down more than 15% year-on-year.
* Germany's energy woes intensify — rising costs and difficulties with the green transition.
* EU institutions begin rolling back ESG reporting requirements — a shift in the regulatory approach.

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: December 2025
posts.excerpt: December 2025 brought significant changes to Poland — a new Archbishop of Krakow, a controversial presidential veto, and leaked plans for healthcare cuts. Internationally, Iran experienced mass anti-government protests, and F1 crowned its new champion with Norris and McLaren triumphant.
posts.body:
December 2025 brought significant changes to Poland — a new Archbishop of Krakow, a controversial presidential veto, and leaked plans for healthcare cuts. Internationally, Iran experienced mass anti-government protests, and F1 crowned its new champion with Norris and McLaren triumphant.

## WORLD

### Politics & Conflicts
* IRAN — mass anti-government demonstrations erupt amid economic crisis and currency collapse (from 28 December). Protests spread to all provinces and continue into 2026.
* Russian General Fanil Sarvarov killed in a car bombing in Moscow.
* Russia fires missiles at the port area of Odessa — at least 8 killed, 27 wounded.
* Series of explosions across Europe — investigations point to Russian intelligence services.
* Further remains of children discovered at the former Mother and Baby Home site in Tuam, Ireland.
* Russian An-22 military transport aircraft crashes in Ivanovo Oblast — 7 fatalities.

## POLAND

### Politics & Law
* President Nawrocki VETOES the Crypto-Assets Act — bringing his cumulative veto count to 17 pieces of legislation.
* LEAKED CORRESPONDENCE from the Ministry of Health to the Finance Ministry — proposed cuts of 10.4 billion PLN; limits on specialist referrals, restrictions on free medicines for children and seniors.
* Maciej Duszczyk steps down as Deputy Minister of the Interior.
* UOKiK (competition watchdog) fines Jeronimo Martins Polska (Biedronka) nearly 105 million PLN for misleading consumers.
* Ministry of National Defence acquires Huta Częstochowa steelworks — a strategic industrial investment.

### Church & Culture
* Cardinal Grzegorz Ryś assumes office as Archbishop Metropolitan of Krakow — solemn investiture at Wawel Cathedral.
* PKP Cargo — CEO Agnieszka Wasilewska-Semail removed from the board.

## SPORT & CULTURE

* LANDO NORRIS wins the 2025 Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship — his first career title. McLaren clinches the Constructors' Championship.
* Magnus Carlsen wins the World Rapid Chess Championship in Doha.
* Alexandra Goryachkina triumphs in the Women's Rapid Chess Championship; Bibisara Assaubayeva wins the Blitz.
* Four Hills Tournament 2025/2026 gets under way — Polish ski jumpers prepare for the season opener.

## ECONOMY & TECHNOLOGY

* Poland: GDP grows 3.8% year-on-year in Q3 2025 — investment up 7.1%; robust growth momentum continues.
* A series of nuclear fusion milestones reported globally — scientists edge closer to key experiments.
* Russian economy under growing pressure from Western sanctions following continued attacks on civilians.
* Poczta Polska stabilises its finances but announces collective redundancies for 2026.

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: January 2026
posts.excerpt: January 2026 opened the new year on an Olympic note — the Winter Games in Milan were in full swing. Poland's economy posted outstanding full-year 2025 results, tensions around Iran continued to mount, and President Nawrocki pressed ahead with a string of presidential vetoes.
posts.body:
January 2026 opened the new year on an Olympic note — the Winter Games in Milan were in full swing. Poland's economy posted outstanding full-year 2025 results, tensions around Iran continued to mount, and President Nawrocki pressed ahead with a string of presidential vetoes.

## WORLD

### Major Events & Politics
* WINTER OLYMPIC GAMES 2026 in Milan and Cortina d'Ampezzo (Italy) — a celebration of winter sport, with ski mountaineering making its Olympic debut as a new discipline.
* The New START treaty between the USA and Russia expires — the end of a key arms control regime; a new phase in the arms race begins.
* Iran — exiled Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi calls for mass mobilisation; millions of Iranians take to the streets. The government responds with a brutal crackdown.
* Trump holds nuclear talks with Iran in Geneva and Oman — no agreement reached.
* Bulgaria adopts the euro — becomes the 21st member of the eurozone (after a delayed timeline).
* BTS returns following completion of all members' mandatory military service — a new album and world tour announced.
* The Rolling Stones announce their 25th studio album.

## POLAND

### Economy
* Poland's GDP for 2025: GROWTH OF 3.6% — the highest figure among large EU economies (EU average: 1.6%). Growth driven by rising investment, strong consumption, and real wages up 6% year-on-year.
* From 1 January, the central NFZ (National Health Fund) e-registration system launches — aimed at cutting waiting times and simplifying appointment booking.
* The deposit return system (kaucyjny) comes fully into force — a revolution in the handling of beverage packaging.
* GDP growth forecasts for 2026: 3.4–3.7% (OECD/NBP). Poland remains the region's growth leader.

### Politics
* President Nawrocki keeps his electoral promise — declines to host Hanukkah celebrations at the Presidential Palace.
* Cumulative presidential vetoes reach 17 pieces of legislation; 117 documents signed — tensions with the coalition government escalate.
* Poland 2050 (Hołownia) faces internal tensions — a potential coalition split looms.
* Strong start for the Warsaw Stock Exchange — broad-based gains across large, mid, and small caps; the Polish zloty strengthens.

## SPORT & CULTURE

* Winter Olympics 2026 — first medals awarded; Polish hopes rest on ski jumpers and biathletes.
* Four Hills Tournament 2025/2026 — concludes on 6 January; Polish competitors fight for the podium.
* Opening rounds of FIFA World Cup 2026 qualifiers (USA–Canada–Mexico); Poland learns its group opponents.

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: February 2026
posts.excerpt: On the very last day of the month, approximately 200 Israeli jets strike nearly 500 targets inside Iran in the largest aerial operation in Israeli history, killing Supreme Leader Khamenei, closing the Strait of Hormuz, and rewriting the geopolitical map of the Middle East in a single night.
posts.body:
February 2026 will go down in history. On the final day of the month, the USA and Israel launched a massive joint military operation against Iran, reshaping global geopolitics in a single moment. Earlier in the month, the world had been captivated by the Super Bowl and the Winter Olympic Games.

## WORLD — TURNING POINT: US & ISRAEL ATTACK IRAN (28 FEBRUARY)

* 28 FEBRUARY 2026 — The USA and Israel launch a joint military operation; Israel names it Operation 'Lion's Roar'; the USA calls it Operation 'Epic Fury'.
* President Donald Trump announces the start of a 'serious war operation' — stated goal: destruction of Iran's nuclear and ballistic missile programmes.
* Approximately 200 Israeli fighter jets strike nearly 500 targets across Iran — THE LARGEST AERIAL OPERATION IN ISRAEL'S HISTORY.
* Explosions rock Tehran and dozens of cities; Iranian airspace is closed and mobile communications disrupted across multiple regions.
* Supreme Leader of Iran Ali Khamenei is KILLED — death confirmed by multiple media outlets.
* Iran announces the closure of the Strait of Hormuz — a direct threat to 20% of global oil and gas supplies.
* Iran retaliates with strikes on Israel and US bases — explosions reported in Dubai and Doha; Kuwait and UAE airports struck.
* Emergency UN Security Council session convened (at the request of China and Russia) — the attack is condemned by Beijing and Moscow.
* Russia pledges support for Iran and shares intelligence data with Tehran.

### Background — Escalation in Preceding Months
* Protests in Iran since 28 December 2025 — the collapse of the rial and surging public anger accelerate the decision to strike.
* Israel had originally planned the operation for mid-2026 but brought the date forward due to the protest dynamics and stalled nuclear talks.
* Negotiations in Oman and Geneva (February 2026) yield no agreement on Iran's nuclear programme.
* 19 February — Trump warns: 'I give it at most 10–15 days before bad things happen.'
* 27 February — the IAEA is unable to verify whether Iran has suspended uranium enrichment.

### Other Key Events
* 8 FEBRUARY — Super Bowl LX at Levi's Stadium, Santa Clara: Bad Bunny headlines the halftime show. Record-breaking viewership figures.
* Winter Olympics 2026 in Milan and Cortina — Polish athletes claim medals; the Games generate global excitement.

## POLAND

### Response to the Iran Strike
* Prime Minister Donald Tusk confirms: Polish citizens and embassy staff in Tehran are safe — Poland is prepared for multiple scenarios.
* LOT Polish Airlines and other carriers suspend flights to the Middle East region — airspace over Iran and Gulf states closed.
* Five Polish nationals previously detained in Iraq are released — the Iraqi Foreign Ministry denies espionage charges; Foreign Minister Sikorski intervenes.

### Domestic Politics
* TV Republika records its first significant ratings decline in a long time — TVP1 returns to the top of the audience rankings.
* Debate continues around the SAFE programme (savings fund) — President Nawrocki weighs a veto or signature.
* Civic Coalition (KO) announces changes to the KPO reform agenda — one reform dropped, a Poland 2050 proposal added.

## ECONOMY & TECHNOLOGY

* Strait of Hormuz closure — global oil prices spike sharply; financial markets gripped by uncertainty.
* Poland: NBP Monetary Policy Council cuts interest rates to 3.75% — further monetary policy normalisation.
* USA assembles its largest military force in the Middle East since the 2003 Iraq War — US military expenditure rises sharply.
